Text:
The moment we go above Rs. 35k, it is always better to go for Iphone,even if it means you need to spend a bit more, which always comes with the state of art advanced processor with better tuning, optimisation and brand value of apple. With power pack performance for a minimum of 4 years.But above all that, the resale value of the device even after 3-4 years. No android operating brands offers that in comparison to iphones. Premium or semi premium android devices lose value quickly and that is what makes it more sensible to invest in an iphone at higher price range. 

That said, i now feel android devices under 30k offer invariably everything you basically want from it so no point really going above that.

Battery capacity over minor thickness compromise anyday. 4700 mah is sufficient but a bigger battery never hurts. Plus realme/one plus is giving a big 5500 mah battery with minor difference in thickness.
Translated:
The moment we go above Rs. 35k, it is always better to go for Iphone,even if it means you need to spend a bit more, which always comes with the state of art advanced processor with better tuning, optimisation and brand value of apple. With power pack performance for a minimum of 4 years.But above all that, the resale value of the device even after 3-4 years. No android operating brands offers that in comparison to iphones. Premium or semi premium android devices lose value quickly and that is what makes it more sensible to invest in an iphone at higher price range. 

That said, i now feel android devices under 30k offer invariably everything you basically want from it so no point really going above that.

Battery capacity over minor thickness compromise anyday. 4700 mah is sufficient but a bigger battery never hurts. Plus realme/one plus is giving a big 5500 mah battery with minor difference in thickness.

Text:
My advice: avoid purchasing phones with curved displays. While curved glass may give a device a premium and stylish appearance, there is a reason most high-end smartphone brands have moved away from this design.

I have purchased a Redmi note 14 pro and regret the decision and yet again Redmi has launched a curved display phone. 

Curved displays are significantly more delicate, and finding suitable tempered glass protection is both difficult and expensive. There are mainly two types of tempered glass available for curved screens. The first uses UV glue, which is relatively cheaper (around ₹200–₹300) but comes with risks, including potential damage to the display and the possibility of voiding the phone’s warranty. The second option is full-glue tempered glass, which offers better protection but is costly, typically ranging from ₹400–₹600.

I regret choosing a phone with a curved display. Had I been aware of these drawbacks—especially the ongoing cost and inconvenience of screen protection—I would have opted for a flat display instead. I hope this review helps others make a more informed decision before purchasing a curved screen smartphone.
Translated:
My advice: avoid purchasing phones with curved displays. While curved glass may give a device a premium and stylish appearance, there is a reason most high-end smartphone brands have moved away from this design.

I have purchased a Redmi note 14 pro and regret the decision and yet again Redmi has launched a curved display phone. 

Curved displays are significantly more delicate, and finding suitable tempered glass protection is both difficult and expensive. There are mainly two types of tempered glass available for curved screens. The first uses UV glue, which is relatively cheaper (around ₹200–₹300) but comes with risks, including potential damage to the display and the possibility of voiding the phone’s warranty. The second option is full-glue tempered glass, which offers better protection but is costly, typically ranging from ₹400–₹600.

I regret choosing a phone with a curved display. Had I been aware of these drawbacks—especially the ongoing cost and inconvenience of screen protection—I would have opted for a flat display instead. I hope this review helps others make a more informed decision before purchasing a curved screen smartphone.

Text:
I personally found Vivo's night time image processing to be extremely heavy handed with copious amounts of oversharpening and noise reduction and the situation is even worse in night mode, oversharpening is through the roof. Consistent pattern is what I have observed with iQOO 13, X200 FE and iQOO Neo 10. 
Video was also very bad in my experience in auto mode but Vivo's pro video mode deserves a mention since it allows completely disabling noise reduction, provides up to 240 fps with manual controls and lens switching while filming in pro video mode.
If OnePlus can make a true compact "flagship" like the Xiaomi 15, then next year, that will be the compact king. For 2025, it's the X15.

Text:
I really like the quality of your video, you definitely deserve more views. I recently made the switch from iPhone (after 8 years of buying almost every new model) to the Xiaomi 15, and I couldn’t be happier. I could write pages about how good this phone is, but for now I just want to share a few complaints and ask for some help.

The biggest issue for me is the 0.6x video mode. Even in 4K, the quality looks worse than on a 5-year-old iPhone 12 Pro. Is there any solution for this?

Other than that, the volume and power buttons feel a bit shaky, not as solid as on an iPhone. These two things are my biggest “problems” with this phone. Also, in some places the Android system UI doesn’t feel as polished as iOS.

Still, even with these minor issues, I’ve found the switch absolutely worth it.
Translated:
I really like the quality of your video, you definitely deserve more views. I recently made the switch from iPhone (after 8 years of buying almost every new model) to the Xiaomi 15, and I couldn’t be happier. I could write pages about how good this phone is, but for now I just want to share a few complaints and ask for some help.

The biggest issue for me is the 0.6x video mode. Even in 4K, the quality looks worse than on a 5-year-old iPhone 12 Pro. Is there any solution for this?

Other than that, the volume and power buttons feel a bit shaky, not as solid as on an iPhone. These two things are my biggest “problems” with this phone. Also, in some places the Android system UI doesn’t feel as polished as iOS.

Still, even with these minor issues, I’ve found the switch absolutely worth it.

I've been using this phone for a month now, and I must say it's a great device for normal usage. It's not a gaming phone, but it has a premium touch and a 4700mAh battery, which is decent. However, if you're used to phones with 5000mAh batteries, you might feel it's draining fast. But if you've previously used phones with smaller batteries (3700-4500mAh), you'll find this one great.

The phone has an amazing hand feel, giving you a premium experience. The Leica camera takes fantastic pictures in good light, almost DSLR-like, but struggles a bit in low light. The cinematic videography is awesome, though!

There are some heating issues, but I've experienced only normal heating, and the cooling chamber does its job in maintaining the temperature. With 65W fast charging, it charges fully in 30-45 minutes, and I charge it twice a day.

Overall, I love this phone for normal use. If you're looking for a gaming phone with massive battery life, you might want to explore other options.

There are some heating issues, but I've experienced only normal heating, and the cooling chamber does its job in maintaining the temperature. With 65W fast charging, it charges fully in 30-45 minutes, and I charge it twice a day.

Text: The phone itself is good, I had it and returned after a few days. The biggest thing, that I didn't like in it is that it does not allow me to set a reduction of blue light as I want. It reduces it slighly but not that good as other brands do. I could not use it at night because the amount of blue light was still high for me. Another thing is the camera. It does a great job, but if you have a child, every 3 photo is usuable and the rest are blury, even with enabled action mode. Maybe I did something wrong, because according to other opinion in this topic, the phone does a great job if you have a children or animals, but mine did not. If you take a photos or non movable objects, then you will be more than happy. Even at night the X15 takes a great shoots. I do not understand why does Xiaomi keep X2.6 as a telephoto lens, when many phones have already X5. The next thing is heat. It is not hot, and I am not a gamer, but the phone is constantly warm when you browse Internet or do simple task. You can feel it even if it fits a case. I am not a fan of this feeling. The last thing, but not least, is the HyperOS in global version. It seems like, it is more like HyperOS lite in comparision to CN version, because there are some stuff that are missing, you do not even have a usable widget for a calendar. HyperOS is also not that fluid as you probably believe. ColorOS or even OriginOS are in this case better. So at the end I just gave up and ordered a Vivo X300.
Translated: The phone itself is good, I had it and returned after a few days. The biggest thing, that I didn't like in it is that it does not allow me to set a reduction of blue light as I want. It reduces it slighly but not that good as other brands do. I could not use it at night because the amount of blue light was still high for me. Another thing is the camera. It does a great job, but if you have a child, every 3 photo is usuable and the rest are blury, even with enabled action mode. Maybe I did something wrong, because according to other opinion in this topic, the phone does a great job if you have a children or animals, but mine did not. If you take a photos or non movable objects, then you will be more than happy. Even at night the X15 takes a great shoots. I do not understand why does Xiaomi keep X2.6 as a telephoto lens, when many phones have already X5. The next thing is heat. It is not hot, and I am not a gamer, but the phone is constantly warm when you browse Internet or do simple task. You can feel it even if it fits a case. I am not a fan of this feeling. The last thing, but not least, is the HyperOS in global version. It seems like, it is more like HyperOS lite in comparision to CN version, because there are some stuff that are missing, you do not even have a usable widget for a calendar. HyperOS is also not that fluid as you probably believe. ColorOS or even OriginOS are in this case better. So at the end I just gave up and ordered a Vivo X300.

Text:
I found the best balance - Leica Vibrant with Leica NAT filter On = natural colours. Also I have set the Metering Preference to Average. 

Also what I noticed, pics taken on Xiaomi 15 in HEIC - when exported looses all it's EXIF data, like when I backup to my Synology Photo there is no data on phone, aperture and such stuff and also no Geolocation data, just the date. Pics taken on my earlier OP 10 Pro, OP 12 and even older Xiaomi 10T Pro in HEIC had no such issues, i.e. all EXIF data was exported intact. So, I now switched back to JPG as format on Xiaomi 15. Ah, also when I copied HEIC from X15 to my notebook, pics have purple tint! Damn, again no such issues with my older phones and HEIC/HEIF. Not sure, maybe with upcoming HyperOS 3 and firmware, this will be resolved - a bug? I put up this topic on reddit couple days ago, no replies! Any comments on this?

Text: Very good detailed review but can't agree on the cameras being top 3. Colours are quite off in many circumstances in photos and the daytime video quality is  oversaturated, way too high in contrast and oversharpened. You have pointed out the contrasty video.  I think the video quality on the Xiaomi 17 is a bit better. Some of Xiaomi's previous phones also had better video quality than the Xiaomi 15.  The Xiaomi 13 for example in daylight produced much more natural video quality.  The contrast at least is a bit lower. The other drawback of the cameras is the jn5 telephoto. The focal range is too short. It's probably the only major Chinese brand in the price category that doesn't have a periscope and zoom performance is behind basically every Chinese brand's standard flagship.
Translated: Very good detailed review but can't agree on the cameras being top 3. Colours are quite off in many circumstances in photos and the daytime video quality is  oversaturated, way too high in contrast and oversharpened. You have pointed out the contrasty video.  I think the video quality on the Xiaomi 17 is a bit better. Some of Xiaomi's previous phones also had better video quality than the Xiaomi 15.  The Xiaomi 13 for example in daylight produced much more natural video quality.  The contrast at least is a bit lower. The other drawback of the cameras is the jn5 telephoto. The focal range is too short. It's probably the only major Chinese brand in the price category that doesn't have a periscope and zoom performance is behind basically every Chinese brand's standard flagship.

Text:
To be honest, am using the Redmi note 13 pro for almost a year now. Not soo much performance has degrade over time. Still switching between 2-3 apps aren't the same level of fluid as they were before. The display is still top notch (1.5k amoled with good enough brightness for outdoor visibility) 
Speakers are good and battery backup is also quite nice (I'd say 6-6.5 hours sot on mixed and 12+ hours on media playback) 
I'd say 60-70 watts of charging on phones are pretty fast and anything above that will degrade the battery and generate heat. 
Performance in games have retained itself even tho I ain't a heavy gamer. You can expect 50-55 fps in genshin impact on low-mid settings. You can do consistent 60 fps gameplay in BGMI (even tho it shouldn't be considered for benchmarking) and CODM. One thing I've realised a lot is that it isn't very thermally balanced, generates heat alot and thermally it throttles. 
Overall, it's good considering I purchased the phone at 17,000 (with bank and credit card offers). Considering to use it for 2-3 more years.
Translated:
To be honest, am using the Redmi note 13 pro for almost a year now. Not soo much performance has degrade over time. Still switching between 2-3 apps aren't the same level of fluid as they were before. The display is still top notch (1.5k amoled with good enough brightness for outdoor visibility) 
Speakers are good and battery backup is also quite nice (I'd say 6-6.5 hours sot on mixed and 12+ hours on media playback) 
I'd say 60-70 watts of charging on phones are pretty fast and anything above that will degrade the battery and generate heat. 
Performance in games have retained itself even tho I ain't a heavy gamer. You can expect 50-55 fps in genshin impact on low-mid settings. You can do consistent 60 fps gameplay in BGMI (even tho it shouldn't be considered for benchmarking) and CODM. One thing I've realised a lot is that it isn't very thermally balanced, generates heat alot and thermally it throttles. 
Overall, it's good considering I purchased the phone at 17,000 (with bank and credit card offers). Considering to use it for 2-3 more years.

Text:
Hey @Nothing, hear me out — we don’t need another giant phone.
We need a compact daily driver — something small, clean, and fast.

Snapdragon 7s Gen 3

LTPO 120Hz display

50MP main + 12MP ultrawide

20MP selfie

5000mAh battery, 35W charging

Price: around $350–$400


That’s all we need for day-to-day life — scrolling, calls, a few photos, and that Nothing aesthetic.
No one’s painting Picasso with their phone or watching HDR movies on a 6-inch screen 😂

Just a simple, beautiful, compact phone that feels premium and runs smooth.
I’d buy it in a heartbeat. 💯
